name: minimal-modular-code
description: Use to keep code MINIMAL and right-sized for AI coding agents. DO — keep an in-progress change minimal (reuse before adding, subtract, avoid the wrong abstraction, check blast radius). REVIEW — audit existing code or a repo for slop (duplication, dead code, over-engineering) and parallel-readiness (coupling, boundaries, gate coverage), then score it. DESIGN — shape right-sized boundaries and work-partitioning so many agents work in parallel, sequence a safe refactor, or explain a principle. Triggers on 'is this over-engineered', 'reduce the slop', 'audit our coupling/boundaries', 'structure this repo for parallel agents'. Do NOT use to wire enforcement gates/hooks/AGENTS.md (use harden-repo-for-coding-agents), for developer API/SDK/CLI surfaces (use dx-audit / dx-design), for product UX (use ux-audit / ui-design), or to tighten prose (use writing-audit).

inspired_by.name: On the Criteria To Be Used in Decomposing Systems into Modules
inspired_by.author: David Parnas
inspired_by.contribution: Information hiding: hide the likely-to-change decision behind a stable interface. The criterion behind module decomposition and the basis for partitioning work across agents along stable seams.

inspired_by.name: A Philosophy of Software Design
inspired_by.author: John Ousterhout
inspired_by.contribution: Complexity is the core problem; deep modules (powerful behavior behind a simple interface) beat many shallow units; minimal means minimal surface, not fewest lines or smallest classes; define errors out of existence.

inspired_by.name: Simple Made Easy
inspired_by.author: Rich Hickey
inspired_by.contribution: Simple (one concept, un-braided) is objective and distinct from easy (familiar/near-at-hand); avoid complecting independent concerns.

inspired_by.name: The Emperor's Old Clothes (ACM Turing Award Lecture)
inspired_by.author: C. A. R. Hoare
inspired_by.contribution: Make a design so simple there are obviously no deficiencies, rather than so complex there are no obvious ones; the first is far harder and the point of the craft.

inspired_by.name: Selected writings (EWD340 The Humble Programmer; EWD1036; EWD1284)
inspired_by.author: Edsger W. Dijkstra
inspired_by.contribution: Keep programs within the limited size of the skull and avoid clever tricks; count lines as 'spent', not 'produced'; elegance is functional, deciding success vs failure.

inspired_by.name: No Silver Bullet: Essence and Accident in Software Engineering
inspired_by.author: Frederick P. Brooks
inspired_by.contribution: Essential vs accidental complexity: minimalism removes accidental complexity but cannot dissolve the problem's inherent difficulty — don't oversell simplicity as a fix for hard problems.

inspired_by.name: The Wrong Abstraction
inspired_by.author: Sandi Metz
inspired_by.contribution: Duplication is far cheaper than the wrong abstraction; prefer duplication until the right abstraction is evident; re-inline a bad abstraction rather than patch it with parameters and conditionals.

inspired_by.name: Yagni
inspired_by.author: Martin Fowler
inspired_by.contribution: Cut speculative features, but YAGNI does not apply to keeping code malleable — the carve-out that separates over-engineering from the boundary investment that enables parallel work.

inspired_by.name: People systematically overlook subtractive changes
inspired_by.author: Adams, Converse, Hales & Klotz
inspired_by.contribution: People default to adding and overlook advantageous subtraction unless explicitly cued; 'what can be removed?' must be a deliberate step, for humans and agents alike.

inspired_by.name: Software Reuse
inspired_by.author: Charles W. Krueger
inspired_by.contribution: Build systems from existing software rather than from scratch; concise, expressive abstractions are what make reuse possible — search and reuse before writing new code.

inspired_by.name: The Thing: Why I Am a Catholic (Chesterton's Fence)
inspired_by.author: G. K. Chesterton
inspired_by.contribution: Do not remove a thing until you understand why it was put there. The guardrail on deletion: subtract deliberately, but gate removal on understanding.

inspired_by.name: Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs
inspired_by.author: Harold Abelson & Gerald Jay Sussman
inspired_by.contribution: Programs are written for people to read, and only incidentally for machines to execute — legibility is the primary audience constraint.

inspired_by.name: Literate Programming
inspired_by.author: Donald E. Knuth
inspired_by.contribution: Reframe coding as explaining to human beings what the computer should do, rather than only instructing the machine.

inspired_by.name: The Elements of Style
inspired_by.author: William Strunk Jr. & E. B. White
inspired_by.contribution: Omit needless words; concise means no wasted elements (a machine no unnecessary parts), not fewest characters — the line between concise and cryptic.

inspired_by.name: Succinctness is Power
inspired_by.author: Paul Graham
inspired_by.contribution: Succinctness measured in semantic elements is power; terseness measured in characters or one-letter names (code golf) is not — the conciseness-vs-cleverness distinction.

inspired_by.name: The Elements of Programming Style
inspired_by.author: Brian W. Kernighan & P. J. Plauger
inspired_by.contribution: Write clearly, don't be too clever; don't sacrifice clarity for small gains in efficiency — write below your maximum cleverness so the code stays debuggable.

inspired_by.name: Learning a Metric for Code Readability
inspired_by.author: Raymond P. L. Buse & Westley R. Weimer
inspired_by.contribution: Readability is a measurable human judgment that correlates with defects and is driven by local features; over-dense lines measurably reduce it — fewer lines is not automatically more readable.

inspired_by.name: A Complexity Measure
inspired_by.author: Thomas J. McCabe
inspired_by.contribution: Cyclomatic complexity counts independent paths; a testability/complexity smell signal that flags code that has grown past easy comprehension.

inspired_by.name: Cognitive Complexity: A new way of measuring understandability
inspired_by.author: G. Ann Campbell
inspired_by.contribution: An understandability-oriented complexity metric (penalize nesting and breaks in linear flow, ignore shorthand that readably condenses) used as a refactor signal, not a target.

inspired_by.name: How Do Committees Invent?
inspired_by.author: Melvin E. Conway
inspired_by.contribution: A system's structure mirrors the communication structure of the organization that builds it; a fleet of loosely-coupled agents will produce loosely-coupled code, fine only if boundaries are enforced.

inspired_by.name: Exploring the duality between product and organizational architectures: A test of the 'mirroring' hypothesis
inspired_by.author: Alan MacCormack, Carliss Baldwin & John Rusnak
inspired_by.contribution: Loosely-coupled organizations produce measurably more modular products — the empirical form of Conway's Law and the default mental model for multi-agent work.

inspired_by.name: Design Rules: The Power of Modularity
inspired_by.author: Carliss Y. Baldwin & Kim B. Clark
inspired_by.contribution: Freeze a small set of stable design rules (interfaces, standards); hidden modules evolve independently beneath them. The contract-and-owner pattern: invest in the interface, keep the implementation cheap and replaceable.

inspired_by.name: Splitting the Organization and Integrating the Code: Conway's Law Revisited
inspired_by.author: James D. Herbsleb & Rebecca E. Grinter
inspired_by.contribution: Modularity is necessary but not sufficient; without explicit, frozen interfaces the collision moves to integration — invest in interface contracts and integration tests when work is split across agents.

inspired_by.name: Design Rule Hierarchies and Parallelism in Software Development Tasks
inspired_by.author: Sunny Wong, Yuanfang Cai, et al.
inspired_by.contribution: Modules in the same design-rule-hierarchy layer are independent and parallelizable; cross-layer dependencies need coordination — the concrete rule for partitioning work across agents.

inspired_by.name: What You See Is What It Does: A Structural Pattern for Legible Software
inspired_by.author: Eagon Meng & Daniel Jackson
inspired_by.contribution: Structure software as independent concepts plus explicit synchronizations so a unit's behavior is readable from the unit — legibility recast for an LLM with a finite context window.

inspired_by.name: Repository Intelligence Graph: Deterministic Architectural Map for LLM Code Assistants
inspired_by.author: Tsvi Cherny-Shahar & Amiram Yehudai
inspired_by.contribution: Handing agents a deterministic build/test/dependency map improves mean accuracy and cuts completion time; the same map both helps one agent and partitions work and detects when two agents' modules touch.

inspired_by.name: CodePlan: Repository-level Coding using LLMs and Planning
inspired_by.author: Ramakrishna Bairi et al.
inspired_by.contribution: Repository-level edits need explicit dependency and change-impact analysis to sequence safely — compute the blast radius of a change before dispatching it.

inspired_by.name: Hierarchical Evaluation of Software Design Capabilities of Large Language Models of Code
inspired_by.author: Mootez Saad et al.
inspired_by.contribution: LLM coupling reasoning is brittle under noise (F1 drops over 50% in noisy open-ended scenarios) — enforce architectural boundaries with structural checks (lint/arch-tests/CI), not agent reasoning.

inspired_by.name: The Friction Is Your Judgment
inspired_by.author: Armin Ronacher & Cristina Poncela-Cubeiro
inspired_by.contribution: Agent speed moves the bottleneck to review; route mechanical defects (format, types, forbidden imports, missing tests) back to the agent via gates, and surface judgment calls (migrations, permissions, dependencies, reliability, architecture/boundary changes) to a human.

metadata.catalog_summary: Keep code minimal and right-sized for AI coding agents: DO (keep a change minimal — reuse, subtract, avoid the wrong abstraction, check blast radius), REVIEW (audit existing code or a repo for slop and parallel-readiness, then score it), DESIGN (shape right-sized module boundaries and work-partitioning, sequence a safe refactor, or explain a principle). Replaces the clean-architecture audit/design pair under a minimalism thesis.
